name: Anton LaVey
description: Anton LaVey (1930-1997) was an American author, occultist, and musician, best known as the founder and High Priest of the Church of Satan. He authored several books, including "The Satanic Bible," outlining his philosophy of LaVeyan Satanism. This philosophy rejects traditional Christian notions of good and evil, instead advocating for individualism, self-indulgence, and a form of ritual magic designed to achieve practical goals. LaVeyan Satanism is atheistic, viewing Satan not as a literal deity but as a symbol of rebellion, individualism, and earthly pleasures. LaVey's teachings emphasize rational self-interest and personal responsibility. He promoted a blend of Nietzschean philosophy, Ayn Rand's objectivism, and elements of ceremonial magic. While LaVey's work gained notoriety and attracted followers, it has also been widely criticized. Mainstream religious scholars and theologians generally dismiss LaVeyan Satanism as a misinterpretation or parody of traditional religious beliefs. Critics often highlight perceived contradictions within LaVey's philosophy and question the authenticity of his rituals. Academics studying new religious movements have analyzed LaVeyan Satanism as a form of religious expression that challenges societal norms and provides a framework for self-empowerment. The Church of Satan continues to exist, though its prominence has fluctuated since LaVey's death.
subtitle: Founder of the Church of Satan

claims.claim: Satan is a symbol of individualism and rebellion, not a deity.
claims.evidence: LaVey's writings in "The Satanic Bible" explicitly state this.
claims.criticism: Traditional religious views consider Satan a real, malevolent entity. LaVey's interpretation is seen as a redefinition, not a reflection of actual religious belief.

claims.claim: Ritual magic can be used to achieve practical goals.
claims.evidence: LaVey describes various rituals in his books, intended to influence events and people.
claims.criticism: Mainstream science rejects the efficacy of magic. The perceived successes of LaVeyan rituals are attributed to placebo effects or confirmation bias.

claims.claim: Self-indulgence and gratification are morally acceptable.
claims.evidence: LaVeyan Satanism promotes the satisfaction of desires as a natural and healthy part of life.
claims.criticism: Many ethical systems emphasize self-control and restraint. Critics argue that unchecked self-indulgence can lead to harmful behaviors and societal problems.

keyFigures.name: Anton LaVey
keyFigures.role: Founder and High Priest of the Church of Satan

keyFigures.name: Michael Aquino
keyFigures.role: Former member of the Church of Satan and founder of the Temple of Set (a splinter group)

keyFigures.name: Arthur Lyons
keyFigures.role: Author of "Satan Wants You: The Cult of Devil Worship in America", a critical examination of Satanism

mythologyConnections: Set (Egyptian deity, adopted by some Satanic groups)

timeline.event: Anton LaVey is born Howard Stanton Levey in Chicago, Illinois.
LaVey founds the Church of Satan in San Francisco, California.
Michael Aquino leaves the Church of Satan and founds the Temple of Set.
Anton LaVey dies in San Francisco, California.
